content_markdown: A gas storage tank of fixed volume $V$ contains $N$ molecules of an ideal gas at temperature $T$. The pressure at kelvin temperature $T$ is $20\text{ MPa}$. $\frac{N}{4}$ molecules are removed and the temperature changed to $2T$. What is the new pressure of the gas?

diagram_metadata.description: No diagram present in the image.

vibe_explanation: Using the ideal gas law in the form $PV = NkT$, we know that $P \propto NT$ since $V$ and $k$ are constant. Initially, $P_1 \propto N \times T = 20\text{ MPa}$. After removing $\frac{N}{4}$ molecules, the new number of molecules is $N_2 = N - \frac{N}{4} = \frac{3}{4}N$. The new temperature is $T_2 = 2T$. Therefore, the new pressure $P_2 \propto \frac{3}{4}N \times 2T = \frac{3}{2}NT$. Since $NT$ corresponds to $20\text{ MPa}$, $P_2 = \frac{3}{2} \times 20\text{ MPa} = 30\text{ MPa}$.
